quick_summary: Firebase is Google's app development platform providing authentication, real-time databases, cloud functions, and hosting as a Backend-as-a-Service. It's popular for rapid prototyping and mobile apps. Interview questions test your understanding of security rules, real-time data sync, and when Firebase is (and isn't) the right choice.

detailed_content.explanation:
Firebase Authentication provides drop-in identity management — email/password, phone, Google, GitHub, and 15+ providers. Think of it as a bouncer at a club door: it verifies who someone is (authentication) but doesn't decide what they can access (authorization). The SDK handles token management, session persistence, and secure token refresh automatically.

The key architectural insight is that Firebase Auth issues JWT tokens that your backend can verify without calling Firebase's servers — the token is self-contained with the user's UID, email, and custom claims. This makes it scalable: your API just verifies the token signature locally using Firebase's public keys.
detailed_content.key_takeaway: Firebase Auth is authentication only — always pair it with Firestore security rules or your own backend middleware for authorization. Never trust the client to enforce access control.
detailed_content.try_it: Set up Firebase Auth with email/password sign-up, then write a Firestore security rule that only allows users to read/write their own documents using `request.auth.uid == resource.data.userId`.
detailed_content.complexity: Time: O(1) for auth operations, token verification is O(1) with cached public keys

pro_tip: In interviews, mention that Firebase is great for MVPs and prototyping but has vendor lock-in concerns — show you understand the tradeoff by discussing how to abstract Firebase behind a service layer so it can be swapped later.
